title: Classes and Interfaces - Effective Java Chapter 4, Items 15-25

questions.question: For a public class, what is the preferred access level for a helper type that is only used inside the package?
questions.explanation: Package-private keeps the type usable inside the package without exporting it as part of the public API.

questions.question: Why are public mutable fields discouraged in exported classes?
options: They freeze representation into the API and prevent invariant enforcement
questions.explanation: A public mutable field exposes implementation details and removes the class's ability to control updates.

questions.question: What is wrong with a public static final array field?
options: Its reference is final, but the array contents remain mutable
The compiler rejects all array constants
It cannot be read from other classes
questions.explanation: The array object itself can still be modified through the returned reference.

questions.question: Which statement about protected members of exported classes is correct?
options: They are part of the exported API and should be rare
They are invisible outside the package
They are always safer than private members
They are only relevant to interfaces
questions.explanation: Protected members are a long-term compatibility promise, so they should be used sparingly.

questions.question: Which rule is part of making a class immutable?
options: Make all fields private and final, and do not expose mutable internals
Use public setters for all fields
questions.explanation: Immutability requires fixed state and no external mutation channel.

questions.question: Why are immutable objects attractive for concurrent code?
options: They are inherently thread-safe and need no synchronization
They eliminate the need for equals
questions.explanation: If state never changes, concurrent readers cannot observe a race on that state.

questions.question: What is the main risk of implementation inheritance across package boundaries?
options: The subclass can break when superclass internals change
The subclass cannot compile at all
questions.explanation: A subclass can depend on superclass behavior that was never part of the public contract.

questions.question: What is the best general replacement for fragile subclassing of a concrete class?
questions.explanation: Composition lets you control the exposed API and avoids tight coupling to internals.

questions.question: What must a class document if it is designed for inheritance?
options: Its self-use of overridable methods and the effects of overriding them
questions.explanation: Subclass authors need to know exactly which overridable methods the superclass calls and when.

questions.question: Why are default methods risky when added to an existing interface?
options: They can silently break preexisting implementations at runtime
They cannot be compiled by javac
They are always ignored by clients
questions.explanation: Existing classes may inherit behavior that does not preserve their invariants.

questions.question: Which is the best use of an interface according to Item 22?
options: To define a type that clients can use to refer to instances
questions.explanation: Interfaces should describe what a type is, not merely provide a bag of constants.

questions.question: What is a skeletal implementation class for?
options: It supplies reusable method bodies while leaving the type as an interface
questions.explanation: Skeletal implementations reduce the work of implementing an interface without forcing inheritance for clients.

modules.name: Tagged classes, member classes, and source files

questions.question: Why are tagged classes discouraged?
options: They force irrelevant fields and switch logic into one brittle type
questions.explanation: A tagged class mixes multiple flavors in one type, which is verbose and error-prone.

questions.question: What is the hidden cost of a nonstatic member class?
options: It stores an implicit reference to its enclosing instance
questions.explanation: That enclosing-instance reference consumes space and can extend object lifetime unintentionally.

questions.question: When is an anonymous class appropriate?
options: For a short one-off use where naming the type is unnecessary
For implementing multiple interfaces at once
For defining a public API class
questions.explanation: Anonymous classes are limited and should stay small; lambdas are usually preferred for function objects.

questions.question: What is the rule for top-level source files in this chapter?
options: Limit each source file to one top-level class or interface
Put all utility classes in one file
Use multiple files only for public classes
questions.explanation: One top-level type per file avoids duplicate-definition and order-dependent compiler behavior.
